The cat led him to a hidden cavern filled with gold and ancient artifacts. With this wealth and the courage he gained through the journey, the youth returned to the kingdom and successfully courted the princess. He remained lifelong friends with the cat, recognizing that the animal's guidance was the true source of his fortune.
moral: Kindness toward animals and a brave spirit can lead to unexpected guidance and great rewards.

title: Capture of the Sampo
summary: The heroes of Kalevala successfully infiltrate Pohjola and steal the Sampo. A thrilling and dangerous chase ensues as Louhi, the Mistress of Pohjola, pursues them with all her might to reclaim the magical mill.
full_text: This legendary tale explores the depths of Finnish folklore. The heroes of Kalevala successfully infiltrate Pohjola and steal the Sampo. A thrilling and dangerous chase ensues as Louhi, the Mistress of Pohjola, pursues them with all her might to reclaim the magical mill. The narrative emphasizes the magical nature of the world and the eternal struggle between order and chaos, reflecting the spiritual beliefs of the ancient North.
moral: Material wealth is fleeting compared to spiritual power.

title: Capture of the Fire-fish
summary: To restore light and warmth, the heroes embark on a perilous journey to the depths of the ocean to capture a magical fire-fish, which is the only source of fire remaining in the world.
full_text: This legendary tale explores the depths of Finnish folklore. To restore light and warmth, the heroes embark on a perilous journey to the depths of the ocean to capture a magical fire-fish, which is the only source of fire remaining in the world. The narrative emphasizes the magical nature of the world and the eternal struggle between order and chaos, reflecting the spiritual beliefs of the ancient North.
moral: The balance of nature must be respected.

title: The Cursed Ring of the Highlands
summary: A traveler finds a gold ring in a mountain stream, only to discover it is cursed. The ring brings immense wealth but also attracts the attention of the Draugrs who previously owned it. The wearer is haunted by whispers and nightmares until they return the ring to the stream from which it came.
full_text: In the ancient lands of Iceland, the story of The Cursed Ring of the Highlands has been told for generations. A traveler finds a gold ring in a mountain stream, only to discover it is cursed. The ring brings immense wealth but also attracts the attention of the Draugrs who previously owned it. The wearer is haunted by whispers and nightmares until they return the ring to the stream from which it came. This legend serves as a reminder of the mysterious forces that once governed the north, where the boundary between the mundane and the supernatural was as thin as a winter's frost. The echoes of these events still linger in the wind and the stone, whispering to those who know how to listen.
moral: One must be careful not to provoke the spirits of the dead.

title: The Curse of the Raven's Eye
summary: A man steals a gemstone from the eye of a carved raven statue in an ancient temple. The gem gives him the ability to see the future, but it also attracts the attention of the raven's spirit, which slowly consumes his sanity until he returns the stone to its rightful place.
full_text: In the ancient lands of Iceland, the story of The Curse of the Raven's Eye has been told for generations. A man steals a gemstone from the eye of a carved raven statue in an ancient temple. The gem gives him the ability to see the future, but it also attracts the attention of the raven's spirit, which slowly consumes his sanity until he returns the stone to its rightful place. This legend serves as a reminder of the mysterious forces that once governed the north, where the boundary between the mundane and the supernatural was as thin as a winter's frost. The echoes of these events still linger in the wind and the stone, whispering to those who know how to listen.
moral: Respect the unseen forces of the world.

title: The Child-Snatcher of the Shore
summary: A cautionary tale about the Qalupalik, a scaly creature that lives beneath the ice and uses a pouch to kidnap children who wander too close to the water's edge. The story blends practical safety advice with supernatural fear to keep children safe from drowning.
full_text: The Qalupalik lives beneath the ice, with green scaly skin, webbed fingers, and a pouch of eider-duck feathers on its back. It moves silently, humming as it swims. The elders tell the children: 'If you wander too close to the water, the Qalupalik will rise, grab you, and stuff you into its pouch. You will never be seen again.' Every child in every coastal village hears this warning. Some say the Qalupalik is real. Some say it is a story to prevent drowning. But every parent knows that the real danger is the water itself — the cold, the currents, the breaking ice. And if the Qalupalik helps keep children away from the edge, it is a spirit worth believing in.
moral: The stories that protect us from danger are valuable whether or not the monster is real.

title: The Chudes Are Tricked by a Boy Pathfinder
summary: A recurring Sámi legend type (Christiansen ML 8000). A band of Chude raiders attacks a Sámi settlement and captures a young boy, forcing him to guide them to other Sámi groups. The boy leads the raiders along a dangerous mountain path. He ties a rope around himself connected to the raiders, then at a critical moment on a steep precipice, he unties himself and cuts the rope, causing the Chudes to pull each other down the slope to their deaths.
full_text: The Chudes came from the east, their swords gleaming in the pale Arctic light. They fell upon a Sámi summer camp, killing the old and capturing the young. Among their prisoners was a boy no more than twelve winters old, with sharp eyes and a quieter voice than the others. 'Guide us to the other Sámi camps,' the Chude leader commanded, 'and you will live.' The boy nodded and began walking. He led them away from the river valleys where the Sámi were hiding and up into the high fells, where the wind never stopped and the rocks were loose underfoot. The Chudes grew tired and suspicious, but the boy kept walking. He produced a rope and tied it around his waist. 'Tie yourselves to this rope,' he said, 'so that if one of us falls, the others can pull him up.' The Chudes tied themselves to the rope in a long line behind the boy. At the narrowest point of the trail, where one side was rock and the other was a drop into nothing, the boy untied the rope from his waist, drew his knife, and cut it. The Chudes, bound together, lost their balance and tumbled down the slope, pulling each other into the abyss. The boy watched them fall, then turned and walked home.
moral: Knowledge of the land is a weapon that no sword can match.
source: Anders Larsen recording 1902; DuBois 1995; laits.utexas.edu; Wikipedia (Chud)

title: The Chudes in Notozero
summary: A Skolt Sámi legend about Chude attackers in the Notozero region. The Sámi use their knowledge of the Arctic landscape and weather to evade and defeat the raiders. The story is part of the extensive cycle of Chude legends reflecting historical conflicts between Sámi and East Slavic raiders. The Chudes are always portrayed as cruel but ultimately outwitted by Sámi cunning.
full_text: At Notozero, where the lakes spread like broken mirrors across the Skolt Sámi territory, the Chudes thought they had found easy prey. They arrived in the long twilight of autumn, when the sun barely rises and the shadows stretch for miles. But the Skolt Sámi had watched them coming from a day's distance. They knew every island, every reed bed, every hidden channel in the lake. They led the Chudes onto the thin ice of the late autumn freeze, where the water beneath was dark and cold. The Chudes, heavy with swords and armor, crashed through the ice one by one. Those who reached the shore found the Sámi had disappeared into the birch forests, leaving only cold campfires and mocking footprints. The Sámi did not need to fight the Chudes. They only needed to let the Arctic do its work.
moral: The Arctic is the deadliest weapon of those who know how to use it.
source: Qvigstad & Saba, Lappiske eventyr og sagn; listed in Sámi Folktales from the Near and Far Worlds

title: The Chudes Are Defeated by a Loon's Beak
summary: A humorous Sámi legend where Sámi outwit Chude raiders using minimal means—in this case, a loon's beak. The story illustrates the theme that superior force can be overcome by cleverness and that even small, humble tools can defeat powerful enemies.
full_text: The Chudes laughed when they saw the Sámi man holding only a loon's beak. 'Is that your weapon?' they roared. The Sámi man said nothing. He waited until the Chudes were close, their swords raised, their faces twisted with cruelty. Then he held up the loon's beak and made the sound of the loon—a long, haunting cry that echoed across the lake. The sound carried to the mountain, where it loosened a rockslide that thundered down upon the Chudes. They were buried under the stones, their swords and shields crushed. Or perhaps the loon's cry summoned the fog, and the Chudes wandered into the bog and drowned. Or perhaps the Sámi simply pointed the beak at the Chudes and they died of laughter. Different versions of the story disagree on the exact mechanism, but they all agree on the result: the Chudes were defeated by a loon's beak, and the Sámi man returned home with his honor and his laughter intact.
moral: Even the smallest and most unlikely tool can defeat the greatest enemy.
source: Qvigstad & Saba, Lappiske eventyr og sagn; Sámi Folktales from the Near and Far Worlds

title: The Creation of the Faroe Islands
summary: Two folk explanations exist for the archipelago's creation. The first says the islands were formed from dirt that gathered under God's fingernails while He created the world and flicked away. The second, more dramatic myth connects to Norse mythology: after the World Serpent Jörmungandr (child of Loki) was defeated, a tooth fell from the dying serpent's mouth into the ocean and became the Faroe Islands. Both accounts are recorded in Faroese oral tradition. The Jörmungandr version connects Faroese identity to the great Norse mythological cycle, while the nail-dirt tale echoes a humble self-perception common in island folklore.
full_text:
Two folk explanations exist for the archipelago's creation. The first says the islands were formed from dirt that gathered under God's fingernails while He created the world and flicked away. The second, more dramatic myth connects to Norse mythology: after the World Serpent Jörmungandr (child of Loki) was defeated, a tooth fell from the dying serpent's mouth into the ocean and became the Faroe Islands. Both accounts are recorded in Faroese oral tradition. The Jörmungandr version connects Faroese identity to the great Norse mythological cycle, while the nail-dirt tale echoes a humble self-perception common in island folklore.

--- 
This story is preserved from an oral tradition. We acknowledge the communities and storytellers who have kept this legend alive through generations.
moral: Great things can come from humble beginnings.
source: The Adventure Seekers; Faroese oral tradition

title: Creation of the Sun and Moon
summary: In the Fourth World of Navajo tradition, First Man, First Woman, and Great Coyote planned the creation of the sun and moon. They summoned Black God (Fire God) from beneath the earth along with Water Sprinkler, House God, and Talking God. Turquoise Boy agreed to become the Sun and White Shell Girl the Moon, but demanded payment: the lives of all beings on earth. First Man heated turquoise on a buckskin with Black God's fire and Turquoise Boy entered it, becoming the Sun (Jóhonaaʼéí, 'The One Who Rules the Day'). White Shell Girl entered white shell and became the Moon (Tłʼéhonaaʼéí, 'The One Who Rules the Night'). They established twelve months with White Shell Girl's whistle of twelve holes. First Angry Coyote, excluded from planning, cursed the arrangement so the moon's months would not match the sun's, bringing irregular seasons and occasional frost and drought.
source: Diné Bahaneʼ; Aileen O'Bryan, The Diné: Origin Myths of the Navajo Indians (1956)

title: The Coming of Death
summary: In Navajo mythology, death came into being when one of the hermaphrodite twins (Nádleeh) stopped breathing after the first day of the Sun's journey. The people abandoned the body in fear, and the next day it was gone — a man looked down through the reed to the Third World and saw the twin sitting by the river. Coyote First Angry proposed a test: he threw a black rock into Black Water Lake, declaring that if it floated, spirits would return and there would be no death; if it sank, death would be permanent. The rock sank. The people learned that looking at the dead is dangerous when the two witnesses also died four days later. Coyote justified his decision by explaining that without death, the world would overpopulate, elders would remain trapped in infirm bodies, and there would be no room for new children.
source: Diné Bahaneʼ; Mescalito et al. in Wikipedia

title: The Circle Kachina and His Wife Become Stars
summary: In Hopi tradition, a maiden grinding corn was approached by the Póngo (Circle) Kachina, who danced at various kivas around the village. She agreed to marry him and went to the Kachina kiva where many different Kachinas gathered. She prepared their food while they prepared her bridal costume. They had two children, also Circle Kachinas. The Hotóto Kachina seduced the wife away to Shongópavi. The Circle Kachina, following her tracks, took his children to the Kachina house. After four days of flute playing at Kíba, the wife emerged from the water in her bridal costume. The Flute priests declared: 'You shall always follow each other this way.' The two ran westward and are still running — they became the stars that pursue each other across the sky, Nangö'sohu, one constantly overtaking and then falling behind the other.
source: H.R. Voth, The Traditions of the Hopi (1905)

title: Coyote Helps Decorate the Night
summary: In this Hopi story about the origins of the night sky, the animals were decorating the darkness. Various animals placed stars and patterns in the sky, but Coyote, growing impatient with the slow, careful work, grabbed a handful of stars and threw them carelessly across the sky. This explains why some stars are scattered randomly while others form orderly patterns. Another version tells that Coyote was given the task of placing the stars but became distracted or deliberately did a poor job, resulting in the Milky Way and the chaotic scattering of stars. The story warns against impatience and the consequences of rushing sacred work.
source: Harold Courlander, People of the Short Blue Corn (1993)

title: The Coyote and the Locust
summary: A Zuni trickster story about Coyote who heard Locust singing and playing his flute on a piñon branch. Coveting the song, Coyote demanded Locust teach it to him. Locust, wary of Coyote's intentions, tricked him by shedding his skin and placing a stone inside the empty shell on the branch. When Coyote returned and Locust refused to sing again, Coyote angrily bit the locust shell — but it was actually a stone wrapped in skin. Coyote broke his teeth, which is why coyotes have broken-looking teeth to this day. The story explains both the physical characteristic of coyotes and warns against anger, impatience, and not minding one's own business. Locusts, for their part, still sometimes shed their skins and leave their likenesses on trees to fool predators.

title: Coyote and Eagle Steal the Sun and Moon
summary: A Zuni story explaining the origin of day and night and the changing seasons. In the beginning, the Sun and Moon were kept locked in boxes by a powerful being who would not release them. The world was in perpetual twilight. Coyote and Eagle, two of the most clever animals, devised a plan. Eagle flew to the being's home with Coyote hidden in his feathers. While the being slept, Coyote crept out, opened the boxes, and Eagle seized the Sun and Moon with his talons. They fled, pursued by the angry being. Eagle rose higher and higher into the sky, placing the Sun and Moon in their proper positions. But in the chaos, Coyote dropped the box containing half the daylight, which shattered. This is why days are shorter in winter than in summer, and why daylight decreases after the summer solstice. The story reflects the Zuni understanding of astronomical cycles and the partnership between sky and earth creatures.

title: The Corn Maidens (Zuni)
summary: In Zuni tradition, the Corn Maidens are sacred beings who embody the spirit of corn. They are associated with Paiyatuma, the god of the dawn and of music, and with the Kachina society. According to the myth, Paiyatuma courted the Corn Maidens, who were beautiful but shy. He played his flute for them, and they danced. Through this union, the sacred connection between corn and the people was established. The Corn Maidens taught the Zuni women how to grind corn, make piki bread, and prepare corn ceremonially. Each color of corn has its own maiden — Blue Corn Maiden, Yellow Corn Maiden, White Corn Maiden, Red Corn Maiden, Black Corn Maiden, and Speckled Corn Maiden. During ceremonies, the Corn Maidens are represented by Kachina dancers who carry ears of corn and bless the fields. The story of the Corn Maidens is central to Zuni agricultural ceremonies and reinforces the sacredness of corn as a gift from the spirit world.
source: Frank Cushing, Zuñi Folk Tales (1901); Ruth Bunzel, Zuni Ceremonialism

title: The Cock and the Mouse (Zuni)
summary: A Zuni folktale of the hybrid type, combining Native and European story elements. The Cock (rooster) and the Mouse were friends living together. Mouse was industrious, gathering food and maintaining their home, while Cock was boastful and lazy. When danger came in the form of a cat or predator, Cock's crowing alerted everyone, but his bravado failed when actual confrontation occurred. Mouse, though small, used cleverness to escape danger. The story humorously contrasts the small, practical Mouse with the showy but ineffective Cock. It teaches that real value lies not in appearance or loudness but in practical skills and quiet diligence. Like many Zuni animal tales, it contains elements of social commentary, humor, and moral instruction wrapped in an engaging narrative.

title: Cheyenne Creation Story
summary: In the beginning, all was water and nothing existed but the Creator Ma'heo'o and aquatic birds. The Creator sent various waterbirds—the loon, otter, beaver, and turtle—to dive to the bottom of the endless water to bring up mud. After several failures, the turtle succeeded in bringing up the primordial mud. From this mud, the Creator formed the earth. Then he created grass, trees, animals, and finally human beings. The Cheyenne emerged from beneath the earth onto the surface, guided by the Creator. The story teaches that all creation is interconnected and that humans must respect the earth as their mother.

title: Comanche Creation: The Dust People
summary: The Great Spirit collected swirls of dust from the four directions—north, south, east, and west—and molded them into strong, powerful figures. He breathed life into them, and the Comanche people were born from the earth itself, infused with the strength of mighty storms and the spirit of the wind. However, a shape-shifting demon formed in the shadows, envious of the people's power. It tormented the Comanche by taking many forms. The Great Spirit cast the demon into a bottomless pit, but before falling, the demon shattered into countless pieces that took refuge in the fangs and stingers of rattlesnakes, scorpions, and spiders, continuing to harm people to this day.

title: Coyote the Comanche Trickster
summary: Coyote is the central trickster figure in Comanche folklore. Unlike the spider tricksters of other Plains tribes, Coyote is a clever but flawed creature who often helps humans while constantly getting into trouble due to greed, dishonesty, and recklessness. Many Comanche storytellers refer to Coyote affectionately as the brother of the Comanches. In one tale, Coyote tries to steal fire from the fire people and is burned, explaining his singed fur. In another, he tricks prairie dogs into leaving their holes so he can catch them, but is himself outwitted by Skunk. Coyote stories teach social values through humor.

title: Crow Morning Star
summary: In Crow belief, the Morning Star (Aapisowaahs) is a heroic figure who lights the night sky. He is the son of Naato'si (the Sun) and Ko'komiki'somm (the Moon), created by the first being Apistotoke. Old Man Coyote traveled to the sky world and brought knowledge of the Morning Star back to the people. The Morning Star is associated with new beginnings, hope, and guidance. In Crow ceremonies, the Morning Star is honored at dawn with prayers and songs. The star's appearance signals the time for certain rituals and serves as a reminder of the Crow's connection to the celestial realm.

title: The Celestial Star Gods
summary: The Skidi Pawnee organized their entire society around the stars. Six major stars represented gods controlled by Tirawa: the North Star (watched over the people), Northeast Star (Big Black Meteoric Star, controlled animals and bison), Southeast Star (Red Star, regulated day and animals), South Star (ruled the land of the dead), Southwest Star, and Northwest Star (both female). The Milky Way contained paths that guided the dead to South Star's dominion. The sun (Shakuru) and moon (Pah) were the last gods placed in the heavens. The four villages of the Skidi were given by the four leading stars.

title: The Creation of Earth and the Great Buzzard (Cherokee)
summary: In the beginning, all was water, and animals lived in the sky world above the stone vault. The water beetle Dâyuni'sï volunteered to explore below and brought up soft mud from the ocean floor, which grew into the earth. The earth was initially soft and flat. The Great Buzzard flew down to survey the land, and his wings striking the ground created the mountains and valleys of Cherokee territory. When the animals saw what the Buzzard had done, they feared he would make the earth too rough, so they called him back, leaving the Cherokee mountains steep and the valleys deep. The sun was set on a track to travel across the sky each day. This foundational myth explains the geography of the Southern Appalachian region and establishes the Cherokee cosmic order.
source: Wikipedia - Cherokee spiritual beliefs; Mooney, Myths of the Cherokee

title: Choctaw Creation: The Emergence from Nanih Waiya (Choctaw)
summary: In the beginning, there was a great mound called Nanih Waiya. The Creator fashioned the first people from the earth within this mound. These first Choctaw emerged from a long, dark cave that led from the center of the mound into daylight. After emerging, they scattered across the land to populate the earth. When a great flood arose, the Choctaw people fled by canoe to an island, guided by a dove. They prospered there but eventually overpopulated the island. They traveled back to the coast of Turtle Island and journeyed for hundreds of years, eventually reaching the Yucatan and finally settling in what is now Mississippi. They rebuilt their sacred Nanih Waiya and found a home for the bones of their ancestors that they had carried throughout their long migration.
source: Wikipedia - Choctaw mythology; Swanton, Source Material Choctaw Indians

title: Chata and Chicksah: The Separation of the Choctaw and Chickasaw (Choctaw/Chickasaw)
summary: Two brothers named Chata and Chicksah led their people from the west, following a sacred pole. Each night they planted the pole in the ground, and each morning they traveled in the direction it leaned. After an immensely long journey, they finally reached a place where the pole stood perfectly upright. There, they laid to rest the bones of their ancestors, which they had carried in buffalo-hide sacks from the original land in the west. The great earthwork mound of Nanih Waiya grew from that burial. Afterward, the land could not support all the people. Chicksah took half the people and departed north, becoming the Chickasaw tribe. Chata and the others remained near Nanih Waiya and became the Choctaw. This shared origin story explains the close relationship between the two tribes.

title: Corn Woman (Creek)
summary: Corn Woman is a central figure in Muscogee Creek mythology. Her origin story varies across traditions: in one version, corn fell from a woman's feet after she rubbed them together; in another, corn poured from her thigh after she scratched it. Many legends end with Corn Woman sacrificing herself to allow corn crops to grow, symbolizing how life comes from death. Corn Woman is closely associated with female fertility and the agricultural cycle. The Green Corn Ceremony, the most important Muscogee ritual, honors her gift and marks the beginning of the new year. The story emphasizes the sacred nature of agriculture and the interconnectedness of women's creative power and the growth of corn, the tribe's staple food.
source: Wikipedia - Muscogee mythology; Grantham, Creation Myths of the Creek Indians

title: The Creek Flood Myth and the Dog's Sacrifice (Creek)
summary: The Muscogee Creek tell a flood story in which the Great Flood covered the entire earth. As the waters rose, the people prayed to the Creator for deliverance. A great dog appeared and offered to sacrifice himself by lying across a creek to dam the water. The dog's noble self-sacrifice allowed the people to cross to higher ground. In gratitude and mourning, the Creator set the dog's spirit in the sky, where it became a constellation. To this day, the Creek honor dogs for their loyalty and self-sacrifice. The myth shares elements with flood stories from many other Native American tribes but is distinctive in its focus on the selfless act of an animal rather than a human or divine hero, reflecting the Creek belief in the spiritual nobility of animals.

title: Chickasaw Creation and the Sacred Pole (Chickasaw)
summary: The Chickasaw creation story tells of their emergence as a people guided by a sacred pole. According to tradition, the Chickasaw and Choctaw were originally one people who migrated from the west. They carried a sacred pole (sometimes described as a dogwood pole) which they planted each night. Each morning, they would travel in the direction the pole leaned. After a long journey, the pole stood perfectly upright at the site of Nanih Waiya mound in Mississippi. There they divided: the Chickasaw, led by Chicksah, traveled north, while the Choctaw, led by Chata, remained. The Chickasaw settled in northern Mississippi, western Tennessee, and northwestern Alabama. Before arriving at Nanih Waiya, they had emerged into this world from a hole in the ground at a place where the sun rises.

title: Chickasaw Flood Myth (Chickasaw)
summary: The Chickasaw flood myth tells of a time when the earth was covered by a great deluge. As the waters rose, the people climbed to the highest ground, but the water continued to rise. Ababinili, the Creator, instructed a man and a woman to build a raft and take on board pairs of all the animals. The flood covered even the tallest mountains for many days. When the waters finally receded, the man and woman found themselves at Nanih Waiya, the sacred mound. From them, the Chickasaw people were reborn. The flood myth shares elements with the Choctaw flood legend, reflecting their shared origins. The story emphasizes renewal and divine mercy — the Creator preserved a remnant of life to begin anew.

title: Corn Woman and the Origin of Corn (Natchez)
summary: The Natchez legend of Corn Woman (also known as Corn-Woman's Son) tells how agriculture came to the people. According to the story, Corn Woman gave birth to a son and from her body came the first corn. In one version, corn poured from her thigh when she scratched it. In another, she sacrificed herself so that corn would grow from her burial place. Her son became the first Great Sun, the divine ruler of the Natchez. The story establishes the connection between female fertility, sacrifice, and agricultural abundance. Corn was the staple crop of the Natchez, and its cultivation was surrounded by ritual and ceremony. The legend teaches that food is a gift that comes through sacrifice and that the people must honor that gift through proper ceremonies.
source: native-languages.org Natchez Legends; Sacred Texts - Corn Woman

title: The Creation of Haida Gwaii
summary: Before the days of the grandfathers, there was nothing but water except a single reef where the supernatural beings lived crowded together. Raven flew about looking for a place to land but could alight nowhere. He flew up to the Sky Land, where he tricked a chief's family by taking the form of a baby. He caused mischief until a stone woman exposed him. The chief sang a magic song and Raven was dropped, falling onto the great waters in his cradle. A grebe appeared and summoned him to the underwater house of Sea-Gull Man. There, Raven was given two rocks: a small speckled one that became Haida Gwaii and a larger black one that became the mainland continent. Raven was instructed to spiral down from the sky, carrying the rocks, and when he reached the islands he made humans from the earth. This creation narrative establishes the sacred origin of Haida Gwaii and the Haida people's deep connection to the land.
source: Haida oral tradition narrated by GwaaGanad (Diane Brown) and James Young; Wikisource Myths and Legends of British North America

title: Cedar Woman (Ts'uu Jaas) and the Cedar Tree (Haida)
summary: In Haida worldview, the Cedar Tree is considered an Ancient Sister called Ts'uu Jaasas. She is a SGaana Kiidads (Supernatural Being) and a physical manifestation of the Haida people's interconnected relationship with the land and sea. The cedar tree is regarded as every woman's older sister, known as Ts'uu K'waayGa. This supernatural being taught the Haida people how to use cedar for clothing, shelter, canoes, and baskets. The cedar tree's importance in Haida culture cannot be overstated: it provided the material for the great houses, the seagoing canoes, the bentwood boxes, and the ceremonial regalia. The Haida legal framework, called the Ts'uu JaasGalang HlGaajuu (Older Cedar Sister) Framework, derives from the teachings of this supernatural being, emphasizing the interconnectedness of all life and the responsibility humans have to care for the natural world.
source: Haida oral tradition and legal scholarship; Terri-Lynn Williams-Davidson

title: The Cannibal Woman Society (Kwakwaka'wakw)
summary: Long ago when totemic beings walked the earth, there was Dzunukwa (Cannibal Woman), a gigantic female being with great wealth and powerful magic who ate people. The other beings became alarmed and decided only Raven could stop her. Raven flew to the North End of the World and recruited a flock of fearsome Cannibal Birds, becoming their leader. He returned with his flock to the great forest where Dzunukwa lived, and they danced and sang her praises. When the dance ended, Dzunukwa found herself bound by powerful magic and could no longer eat flesh — the only things she could eat were nuggets of native copper. This story establishes the origin of the Hamatsa (Cannibal Woman) Society, the most important secret society in Kwakwaka'wakw culture. The society's winter ceremonies reenact the capturing and taming of the cannibal spirit, transforming its destructive power into constructive social and spiritual energy for the community.
source: Kwakwaka'wakw oral tradition; Charles M. Nelson

title: Changer and the Creation of the World (Coast Salish)
summary: In Coast Salish tradition, dukʷibəɬ the Changer (also known as Xe'las or Xelas) walked the earth when the world was not yet as it is today. Sometimes he appeared as a brilliant light from the East, sometimes as a young man, sometimes as several young men, and sometimes as an invisible old person who knew everything. The Changer went everywhere, preparing the world for the First People who were yet to come. He transformed inappropriate things into useful ones: he turned rocks that beat people over the head into hammers, sticks that struck people into fish spears, and dangerous beings into animals, birds, and landscape features. The Changer also sent a flood to cleanse the earth when things went wrong. All living things were destroyed except one woman and one dog, who fled to the top of Tacobud (Mount Rainier). From them, the next race of people was born. The Changer's work established the Coast Salish understanding of the world as a place shaped by a powerful transformer who continues to watch over the land and its people.
source: Coast Salish oral tradition; Hibulb Cultural Center; Franz Boas; Brian Thom, Transformer Stories

title: Crane and Changer (Coast Salish)
summary: As told by Snoqualmie Charlie, this story recounts how dukʷibəɬ the Changer came upon a man with very long legs standing by the shore, getting hit over the head by rocks. The Changer asked what he was doing, and the man explained he was trying to get food but the rocks kept hurting him. The Changer took the rocks, declared they would be hammers for the people, and knocked them together, transforming them into useful tools. Then the long-legged man picked up a stick to use as a fish spear, but it too hit him over the head. The Changer took the stick, broke it in two, and declared they would be fish spears. He gave the transformed items to the man and walked away, continuing his journey of preparing the world. The long-legged man was transformed into the Crane, who to this day stands on long legs at the water's edge, using his beak as a fish spear. This is one of many stories showing how the Changer shaped the world by transforming flawed ancestors into the animals and birds we know today.
source: Hibulb Cultural Center; Snoqualmie Charlie oral tradition

title: The Child and the Cannibal (Nuxalk)
summary: This Nuxalk legend tells of a magical boy who defeats a man-eating monster that terrorized the people. A cannibal giant (Boqs) had been stealing children from a Nuxalk village, carrying them off to his remote mountain lair to devour. The people were helpless against the monster's supernatural strength and ferocity. A young boy, born with extraordinary powers and protected by supernatural helpers, volunteered to confront the cannibal. Using cleverness, courage, and his spirit-given abilities, he tracked the monster to its den. He outwitted the slow-witted giant, leading it into traps and finally killing it with the help of the forest spirits. He freed all the captured children and returned them safely to the village. The boy became a great chief and his descendants established one of the Nuxalk clans. This is part of a broader Nuxalk tradition of hero-child stories where young protagonists overcome powerful supernatural adversaries, demonstrating that age and size are less important than courage and spiritual power.
source: Nuxalk oral tradition; native-languages.org; Franz Boas

title: Coyote and the Creation of the Tribes (Chinook/Chelan)
summary: Long ago, the Great Chief Above made the world, then the animals and birds. After twelve moons, the animal people gathered. Bluejay, Meadowlark, and Coyote complained about their names, but the Creator declared that names would stay as given. The Creator told Coyote he would be the head of all creatures and given power to keep peace. As the Creator foretold, things became bad — mountains swallowed creatures, winds blew them away. Coyote stopped the mountains and winds, rescued the creatures, and made laws for natural forces. When Coyote's work was done, the Creator told Coyote he would not make the first human. Instead, the four Wolf Brothers took over. The youngest Wolf Brother killed Beaver and cut it into twelve pieces according to the Creator's instructions. The pieces were given to different animal people who carried them to different regions, where each piece became a different Indian tribe: the Nez Perce from the liver, the Spokane, Lake people, Flathead, and others from different organs. The blood became the Blackfeet. This story explains the origin of the twelve tribes of the Columbia Plateau region.

title: The Conjure Doctor Who Outwitted the Master
summary: On a Virginia plantation, the master hired a notorious slave driver to break the spirit of the enslaved people. Whippings and punishments only increased, and the people grew desperate. An old conjure doctor named Uncle Monday was called from a neighboring plantation. Uncle Monday walked through the quarters, speaking to the people and gathering roots and herbs from the forest. He prepared a special mojo bag and buried it at the crossroads near the plantation gate. That night, the slave driver could not sleep; snip he was tormented by nightmares of his victims. The next day, he was thrown from his horse for no reason. Within a week, he fled the plantation, claiming it was haunted. The master, furious, accused Uncle Monday of witchcraft and threatened to have him whipped. Uncle Monday calmly pointed at a tree stump, and it burst into flames. The master never touched him. Uncle Monday taught the people that conjure was their protection, passed from Africa, and that no master could ever truly break what the spirits protected. The tale embodies the power of conjure as a form of psychological and spiritual resistance against the violence of slavery.
source: African American oral tradition; snip Charles W. Chesnutt, The Conjure Woman (1899); snip Zora Neale Hurston collections

title: The Crossroads Spirit
summary: In African American conjure tradition, the crossroads is the most spiritually charged place—the intersection where the physical world meets the spirit world. The story tells of a young man who went to a crossroads at midnight seeking power. He was instructed by a rootworker to bring a black chicken, a bottle of whiskey, and nine silver dimes. At the crossroads, he was to draw an X on the ground, light a black candle, and wait. The spirits would test him with terrors, but he must not run. According to some accounts, a figure would appear—sometimes the Devil, sometimes an old man, sometimes a faceless presence—and offer a deal: power and fortune in exchange for his soul. The crossroads spirit could teach the secrets of conjure, give winning lottery numbers, or make an enemy sick. But the price was always steep. Those who succeeded at the crossroads gained power; snip those who failed lost their minds or their lives. The crossroads story blends African spiritual traditions (where crossroads are home to the trickster god Eshu-Elegba) with the American experience of desperate people seeking power in a world that denied them any.
source: African American Hoodoo tradition; snip Zora Neale Hurston, Mules and Men (1935)

title: Cunjah Maum and the Healing Roots
summary: On a rice plantation along the South Carolina coast, an old Gullah woman named Cunjah Maum was known as the most powerful rootworker in the Lowcountry. When the master's daughter fell ill with a mysterious fever that no doctor could cure, the master reluctantly sent for Cunjah Maum. She came to the big house and asked for three things: a lock of the girl's hair, water from the spring that flowed east, and privacy. Cunjah Maum ground roots and leaves into a paste, mixed it with the spring water, and sang songs in an African language over the mixture. She rubbed it on the girl's forehead and feet and prayed to ancestors she could see standing in the corners of the room. Within three days, the girl recovered completely. The master offered Cunjah Maum money, but she refused—she asked instead that her grandson be taught to read. The master, grateful, agreed. Cunjah Maum's story embodies the Gullah belief in the power of rootwork to heal not just the body but to negotiate justice in a world where Black people had little power. Her refusal of money and demand for literacy represents the deeper value of knowledge over wealth.
source: Gullah Geechee oral tradition; snip Drums and Shadows (Georgia Writers' Project, 1940)

title: The Children of La Llorona — The Weeping Children
summary: A poignant variant of the La Llorona legend tells that the spirits of the children she drowned did not pass on but remain as eternal, weeping children along the same waterways. Known sometimes as Los Hijos de la Llorona, these child spirits are said to appear as small, translucent figures playing near rivers and canals at dusk. Unlike their mother, who wails in anguish, the children are sometimes described as playful but sad, trying to lure living children to join them in the water. In some New Mexican versions, the children appear to warn other children away from dangerous waters. This variant transforms the story from one of pure horror into a tragedy of intergenerational trauma, where the sins of the mother echo through the spirits of the innocent. In Chicano literature and art, these children have been reclaimed as symbols of lost innocence and the enduring wounds of colonization.
source: New Mexican and borderlands folklore; oral traditions

title: The Curse of the Dolls — La Isla de las Muñecas
summary: The legend of the Island of the Dolls (La Isla de las Muñecas) in the Xochimilco canals near Mexico City is a modern legend that has spread widely through Mexican-American communities. According to the story, a man named Don Julián Santana found the body of a drowned girl in the canals and, haunted by her spirit, began collecting dolls and hanging them from trees to appease her soul. Over 50 years, thousands of decaying, mutilated dolls accumulated on the island, their blank eyes and torn limbs creating an atmosphere of profound unease. After Don Julián drowned in the same canal in 2001, the legend grew: visitors claim the dolls move their heads, blink, and whisper at night. The legend speaks to themes of death, childhood, and the thin boundary between the natural and supernatural worlds. For Mexican-Americans, the story resonates as a uniquely Mexican contribution to global horror folklore.
source: Mexican urban legend; Xochimilco oral tradition; travel and paranormal accounts

title: The Creation of the World (Yoruba)
summary: In the beginning, there was only water and chaos. Olodumare, the Supreme Being, sent Obatala down from the heavens with a calabash filled with soil, a rooster, and a palm nut. Obatala poured the soil onto the waters, and the rooster spread it to create dry land at Ile-Ife, the cradle of existence. He planted the palm nut, which grew into a great tree with sixteen branches, symbolizing the sixteen sacred Odù Ifá. Olodumare then breathed life into the clay figurines Obatala had molded, creating the first humans. Thus Ile-Ife became the sacred birthplace of Yoruba civilization and the dwelling place of the Orishas.
source_references: Johnson, Samuel. The History of the Yorubas. Routledge, 1921; Verger, Pierre. Dieux Yoruba. Éditions africaines, 1954

title: The Creation of the Niger River (Mande)
summary: Long ago, the Niger River flowed only underground. A heroic hunter named Fara Makan tracked a wounded antelope for seven days without water. When he was dying of thirst, he shot an arrow into the ground, and water burst forth, creating the Niger River. The river then wound its way across the Sahel, bringing life to the lands of Mali, Niger, and Nigeria. The Djoliba (the Mande name for the Niger) became the lifeblood of the Mali Empire, enabling agriculture, trade, and transport. Sundiata Keita's empire was built along this great river, and the river's annual flood is still celebrated as a gift from the spirits. To this day, fishermen and farmers along the Niger honor the river with offerings.
source_references: Hunwick, John O. Timbuktu and the Songhay Empire: Al-Saʿdī's Tārīkh al-Sūdān. Brill, 1999; Vansina, Jan. Oral Tradition as History. James Currey, 1985

title: The Creation of the World (Fon/Dahomey)
summary: Before the world existed, there was only water, darkness, and the supreme deity Nana Buluku. Nana Buluku gave birth to twins: Mawu, the goddess of the moon and night, and Lisa, the god of the sun and day. Together, Mawu-Lisa created the world. They then gave birth to children who became the heads of the great vodun pantheons: Sogbo (thunder), Sagbata (earth), and Agbe (sea). Mawu created the first humans from clay and taught them how to live in harmony with nature. Lisa brought fire, metal, and civilization. Their androgynous union represents the balance of opposites that governs the universe. This creation story is central to the Vodun religion that spread from Dahomey (Benin) to Haiti, Brazil, and Cuba.
source_references: Law, Robin. The Oyo Empire c.1600–c.1836: A West African Imperialism in the Era of the Atlantic Slave Trade. Clarendon Press, 1977; Ajayi, J.F. Adele. Yoruba Traditions and the Rise of the Oyo and Benin Kingdoms. Journal of the Historical Society of Nigeria, 2(2): 1961

title: Chiwara: The Antelope Spirit Who Taught Agriculture
summary: The Bambara people of Mali revere Chiwara, a mythical half-human, half-antelope being who taught humans the fundamentals of agriculture. Chiwara used its antlers and a pointed stick to dig into the earth, making it possible for humans to cultivate the land. The people watched the antelope and followed its example to create their own farms, becoming expert farmers. Their harvests grew so bountiful that they began wasting the grain, taking abundance for granted. Disappointed, Chiwara buried itself deep into the earth and disappeared. The elders, regretting the loss of their great teacher, ordered that masks be made in Chiwara's memory. To this day, Bambara dancers wear Chiwara headdresses in ceremonies that honor the agricultural spirit, leaping and turning like antelopes to keep the teaching alive.
source_references: Roy, Christopher D. Art of the Upper and Lower Niger Rivers, c. 400 B.C.–1900. Ethnologisches Museum Berlin, 1987; Strother, Susan L. Inventing masks: Agency and history in the art of the Central Pende. University of Chicago Press, 1998

title: The Creation of Lake Issyk-Kul
summary: An alternate legend describes the origin of Issyk-Kul ('Warm Lake') as the tears of the heavens. An old man and his beautiful daughter lived on the shores of a small river. When invaders came, the daughter prayed to Tengri for salvation. The sky god answered by sending a flood that drowned the invaders but also the old man. The daughter's endless tears of grief filled the valley, forming the vast lake that never freezes despite its altitude. The warm waters represent the mother's eternal love and the father's enduring protection. Even today, Kyrgyz herders say the lake's surface reflects the faces of those who died in the flood.

title: The Creation of the Karakum Desert
summary: According to Turkmen legend, the Karakum ('Black Sands') Desert was created by a battle between good and evil forces. The devil challenged the creator god to a contest — who could cover more land in one night. The creator god began covering the land with fertile soil, but the devil raised giant sandstorms. The creator god, realizing the devil's trick, scattered black stones across the sand to stop his advance. These stones became the desert's namesake 'black sands.' Another legend says that a giant serpent slept beneath the land, and when it turned in its sleep, it churned the earth into sand. The few oases that survive in the desert were created where the tears of angels fell, grieving for the land's desolation. The Bakhshi singers still perform tales of how the Turkmen people learned to thrive in this harsh environment.

title: Chura-Batyr: The Hero of Kazan
summary: Chura-Batyr is the heroic epic of the Tatar people, set in the mid-16th century during the Kazan Khanate's struggle against the expanding Moscow state. Chura is a batyr (hero) who defends Kazan against Russian invasion. The epic contains ancient motifs including Chura's miraculous birth to elderly parents after years of childlessness, his heroic childhood, his journey to Kazan with the blessing of a holy elder, and his service to the khan. The Tatar version emphasizes Chura's resistance against Ivan the Terrible's forces. In the most moving section, Chura is betrayed and killed through treachery, but his sacrifice inspires the Tatar people to continue fighting. The epic is a powerful expression of Tatar national identity and resistance, surviving in multiple versions among Kazan Tatars, Crimean Tatars, Nogais, and Kazakhs.

title: Chyskhaan, the Snow Spirit
summary: The Yakut legend of Chyskhaan, the powerful spirit of winter and cold who rules over the frozen landscape of Siberia. Chyskhaan is depicted as a towering figure with icy breath that can freeze anything instantly and a heart made of permafrost. He is both feared and respected by the Sakha people, as he governs the long, brutal winters that define life in Yakutia. Offerings are made to appease him before the onset of winter. In modern Sakha culture, Chyskhaan has been adopted as a regional Father Frost figure (similar to Ded Moroz) while retaining his ancient animistic origins as the personification of the extreme cold that can reach -60°C.

title: The Creation of Lake Baikal
summary: A Buryat legend explaining the origin of Lake Baikal, the world's deepest and oldest lake. According to the myth, there was once a great earthquake — the mightiest ever seen — which created a massive crack in the earth's surface. Fire and lava erupted from the crack, threatening to destroy everything. The Buryat people cried out 'Bai Gal! Bai Gal!' meaning 'Stop Fire! Stop Fire!' and the flames ceased. Water then filled the vast depression, forming Lake Baikal. The Buryats consider Baikal a sacred living entity, calling it 'Baikal the Father.' The lake is home to countless spirits and is the dwelling place of powerful deities including the water spirit Usan Khatan and the great dragon-like Luus spirits.

title: The Cosmic Egg Creation (Bon Tradition)
summary: The pre-Buddhist Tibetan Bon creation myth describing the origin of the universe from a cosmic egg. According to this tradition, in the beginning there was only emptiness. From the void emerged five pure lights, which condensed into a luminous egg. From this cosmic egg hatched the primordial being, who then created the world by separating sky from earth. The egg's white shell became the heavens, its yolk became the earth, and the liquid within became the oceans. Mountains, rivers, and all living beings arose from different parts of the egg's contents. This creation account is preserved in the Bon Kangyur and Tengyur sacred texts and represents Tibet's indigenous cosmology predating the introduction of Buddhism in the 7th century.

title: Chhinnamasta — The Self-Decapitated Goddess
summary: Goddess Chhinnamasta (the 'Self-Decapitated One') is a powerful Tantric deity worshipped primarily in Bengal and parts of eastern India. The legend tells that once, while bathing in the Mandakini River with her attendants Dakini and Varnini, Parvati (in her form as Chhinnamasta) was overcome by a ravenous hunger that her attendants also felt. When asked to satisfy their hunger, Parvati severed her own head with a sword and held it in her hand. Three jets of blood spurted from her severed neck — one feeding her own mouth, and two feeding her attendants. The iconography shows her standing in a battlefield, holding her severed head, drinking her own blood. The myth symbolizes the goddess's power to transcend death and her role as both destroyer and sustainer of cosmic energy (Shakti).
source: Tantric Hindu traditions, Shakta Puranas, Bengali folklore
moral: Wisdom and wit prevail where strength alone cannot

title: The Creation of the Kathmandu Valley by Manjushri
summary: According to the Swayambhu Purana, the Kathmandu Valley was once a great lake called Nagavasahrada, inhabited by serpent beings (Nagas). On the lake floated a miraculous self-arisen flame (Swayambhu Jyotirupa) emanating from a thousand-petalled lotus. The Bodhisattva Manjushri, meditating on the Five-Peaked Mountain in China, became aware of the sacred light and travelled to the lake with his two wives, Kesini and Upakesini. Using his mighty sword Chandrahasa, Manjushri cut through the Chobhar Gorge, draining the lake and creating the fertile Kathmandu Valley. He persuaded Karkotaka, the King of Nagas, to remain in a smaller lake (Taudaha) as guardian of the valley's wealth. The lotus settled on Swayambhu Hill, where the great Swayambhu Stupa now stands. Manjushri then founded the first settlement, Manjupattan.
moral: Wisdom and wit prevail where strength alone cannot

title: Chimi Lhakhang and the Divine Madman Drukpa Kunley
summary: Chimi Lhakhang (the Fertility Temple) in Punakha district, Bhutan, is associated with Drukpa Kunley (1455–1529), the 'Divine Madman' — a Buddhist monk famous for his outrageous methods of teaching. When three demonesses terrorized the Dochula Pass, Drukpa Kunley pursued them to Lobesa Hill, where the chief demoness Loro Duem transformed into a dog. Kunley subdued her using his phallus — called the 'Flaming Thunderbolt of Wisdom' — and buried her under a black chorten, naming the site 'Chimi' (meaning 'no dog'). The temple, built in 1499, is a fertility pilgrimage site where childless couples receive blessings by being struck on the head with a 10-inch ivory and wood phallus. Drukpa Kunley is credited with introducing phallus paintings on Bhutanese houses to drive away evil spirits. His dog Sachi is also enshrined with him in the temple.
source: Drukpa Kunley biographies, Chimi Lhakhang temple records
moral: Wisdom and wit prevail where strength alone cannot

title: Changó, the Lord of Thunder and Fire
summary: Changó (Shangó) is the powerful orisha of thunder, lightning, fire, and war in Cuban Santería/Lucumí, syncretized with Saint Barbara in Catholic tradition. According to myth, Changó was once a king of the Yoruba city of Oyo who was deified after death. He wields a double-headed axe and dances with fiery intensity. Changó's legends tell of his battles, his many lovers including Oshún and Oyá, and his terrible temper. The batá drums are sacred to him, and his number is 6. A famous patakí (sacred story) tells how Changó received his powers from Obatalá and how he used thunder to defeat his enemies. In Cuba, Changó became a symbol of resistance and masculine power for enslaved Africans.
moral: Wisdom and wit prevail where strength alone cannot

source_references.title: Folk Literature of the Garifuna People of the Americas
source_references.author: Luis J. González

title: The Chickcharney of Andros Island
summary: The Chickcharney is a legendary creature from Bahamian folklore, specifically from Andros Island. It resembles a small, elf-like being with owl-like feathers, glowing red eyes, three fingers on each hand, and three toes on each foot. It walks upright and is said to whistle softly as it moves. According to tradition, how a person treats the Chickcharney determines whether luck or misfortune follows. Construction workers and lumberjacks on Andros Island would leave small offerings of food at the edge of their work sites as gestures of respect. Those who showed respect had safe workdays and found missing tools returned neatly nearby. Those who mocked the old warnings suffered accidents, illness, and misfortune. The Chickcharney embodies the principle that the forests hold memory, presence, and power deserving of respect.
source: FolktalesAmerica.com; Wikipedia; Discover Bahamas; Daniel J. Crowley, Folk-Tales of Andros Island, Bahamas
moral: Not all that seems beautiful is safe

source_references.title: Islands of the Bahamas: A Folklore Survey

title: The Cave of Caguana: Taino Creation
summary: According to Taino mythology, all human life began in a sacred cave called Cacibajagua (Cave of the Jagua) on the island of Haiti (Hispaniola). The first humans emerged from this cave, while the gods dwelt in the Cave of the Serpent and the Cave of Coaybay. The deity Iguanaboína was among the first beings from the river. The Taino believed their ancestors originated from this cave system, with different groups emerging at different times. Those who emerged late were said to have been turned into stone by the morning sun — explaining certain rock formations. The cave of Caguana in Puerto Rico contains some of the most important Taino petroglyphs, depicting Atabey in her birthing position, marking it as a sacred ceremonial center for areítos (ceremonial dances and songs).
moral: The world is shaped by powerful forces both creative and destructive

title: The City of Brass
summary: Caliph Abd al-Malik sends Emir Musa and his party across the Sahara to find the legendary City of Brass, built by King Solomon to imprison rebellious jinn. They encounter a brass horseman statue that points the way, a chained Ifrit imprisoned in a pillar, and an ancient city with walls eighty cubits high and twenty-five gates, eerily preserved but inhabited only by corpses. Inside lies the embalmed princess Tadmurah of the Amalekites, her treasure guarded by animated statues. Greedy Talib ignores warnings and is decapitated by guardian statues. The party retrieves brass bottles containing imprisoned jinn and returns to Damascus.

title: The Contendings of Horus and Set
summary: The epic struggle between Horus, the son of Osiris, and Set, the murderer of Osiris, for the throne of Egypt is one of the most dramatic myths from ancient Egypt. After Osiris descended to the underworld, his son Horus came of age and demanded the kingship that was rightfully his. Set, Osiris's brother, refused to yield. The case was brought before the Ennead, the council of gods, presided over by Geb or Ra. The trial lasted eighty years. The conflict was both legal and violent: the gods debated endlessly while Horus and Set engaged in terrible battles. Set tore out Horus's eye; snip Horus castrated Set. Isis used cunning and magic to aid her son, at one point transforming into a beautiful woman to trick Set into condemning himself. Thoth served as scribe and mediator. Eventually the gods ruled in Horus's favor, and he became king of Egypt. The myth established the principle of divine kingship: every pharaoh was considered the living Horus. Set was not destroyed but was given dominion over the desert and foreign lands, representing chaos kept at bay by order.
source: Wikipedia - Osiris myth; snip Pinch - Handbook of Egyptian Mythology
source_references: Shirley, Greg. The Ancient Egyptian Contendings of Horus and Seth. In Ancient Egyptian Literature: Theory and Practice, edited by Roland Enmarch and Vernus. Cambridge University Press, 2013; Lichtheim, Miriam. Ancient Egyptian Literature. Vol. 1. University of California Press, 1973

title: The Clever Girl of Kairouan
summary: A Tunisian folktale from the ancient city of Kairouan about a poor man and his brilliant daughter. When famine strikes, the man seeks help from a corrupt judge who sets an impossible condition: 'You must come to me neither dressed nor undressed, neither walking nor riding, neither by day nor by night.' The man's daughter solves the riddle: she wraps herself in a fishing net so she is neither clothed nor naked, rides a donkey with her feet dragging so she is neither walking nor riding, and arrives at dawn—the moment between night and day. When the judge tries to swindle her father, she outsmarts him in court with her quick wit, exposing his corruption and securing justice for her family. The story became famous throughout Tunisia as a symbol of how intellect and moral courage can overcome corrupt authority, and is still told to emphasize the values of wisdom and justice in Maghrebi oral tradition.
source: FolktalesAfrica.com - The Clever Girl of Kairouan
source_references: Cardaire, Marie-Joséph. L'Artisanat artistique au Togo. Mision catholique, 1965; Leloup, Henri. Art et mythologie du Togo. Centre culturel français, 1966
